front: Eyewitness ID: what two distinct federal doctrines govern challenges to an eyewitness identification?
back: (1) The Sixth Amendment right to counsel at a post-charge corporeal lineup/showup (Wade/Gilbert); and (2) the Due Process Clause bar on identifications produced by unnecessarily suggestive, police-arranged procedures creating a substantial likelihood of irreparable misidentification (Stovall/Biggers/Manson). They are independent inquiries.

front: Eyewitness ID (due-process branch): even when a procedure is unnecessarily suggestive, what single consideration controls admissibility?
back: Reliability is the linchpin. A suggestive ID is still admissible if it is reliable under the totality of the circumstances; suppression is the exception, not the rule.

front: In United States v. Wade (1967), what did the Court hold about a post-indictment lineup?
back: A post-indictment lineup is a critical stage of the prosecution at which the accused has a Sixth Amendment right to counsel; counsel's absence may taint a later in-court identification unless that in-court ID has an independent source.

front: Under United States v. Wade, after an uncounseled post-charge lineup, what allows an in-court identification still to be admitted?
back: An independent source for the in-court ID — i.e., the witness's identification rests on observation untainted by the uncounseled lineup, not on the lineup itself.

front: In Gilbert v. California (1967), what rule governs testimony that the witness identified the accused at an uncounseled post-charge lineup?
back: It is excluded per se — a strict rule with no harmless-error and no reliability cure — to deter Sixth Amendment violations at lineups.

front: Eyewitness ID distinction: how does Gilbert's per se exclusion differ from the due-process suggestiveness branch on the question of reliability?
back: Gilbert's uncounseled-lineup defect cannot be cured by reliability — the lineup-ID testimony is excluded outright. By contrast, in the due-process branch (Biggers/Manson) reliability can rescue even a suggestive ID.

front: Under Kirby v. Illinois (1972) (plurality), when does the Sixth Amendment right to counsel at an identification attach?
back: Only at or after the initiation of adversary judicial criminal proceedings (formal charge, preliminary hearing, indictment, information, or arraignment). A pre-charge identification is not a critical stage.

front: Kirby v. Illinois (the attachment language): the right attaches at "the initiation of {{c1::adversary judicial criminal proceedings}}—whether by way of formal charge, preliminary hearing, indictment, information, or arraignment."
back: adversary judicial criminal proceedings

front: In United States v. Ash (1973), is there a Sixth Amendment right to counsel at a post-indictment photographic display?
back: No. A photographic display is not a trial-like confrontation because the accused is not present, so it is not a critical stage.

front: United States v. Ash held: "the Sixth Amendment does not grant the right to counsel at {{c1::photographic displays}} conducted by the Government for the purpose of allowing a witness to attempt an identification of the offender."
back: photographic displays
cite: United States v. Ash, 413 U.S. at 321

front: Eyewitness ID distinction (Wade/Gilbert vs. Ash): why does the Sixth Amendment right to counsel reach corporeal lineups but not photo arrays?
back: A corporeal lineup is a trial-like confrontation where the accused is present and may be prejudiced without counsel (Wade/Gilbert). A photo array involves no trial-like confrontation because the accused is not present, so no counsel right attaches (Ash).

front: In Stovall v. Denno (1967), what did the Court hold about an unnecessarily suggestive confrontation?
back: A confrontation that is unnecessarily suggestive and conducive to irreparable mistaken identification can violate due process; admissibility turns on the totality of the circumstances.

front: Eyewitness ID (facts→case): A single-suspect hospital bedside showup was conducted because the only witness was in critical condition and might not survive; the Court held the showup did not violate due process. Which case?
back: Stovall v. Denno (1967) — the bedside showup, though suggestive, was justified by the witness's critical condition under the totality of the circumstances.

front: In Neil v. Biggers (1972), what did the Court hold about an unnecessarily suggestive identification?
back: Even an unnecessarily suggestive identification is admissible if, under the totality of the circumstances, it is nonetheless reliable; reliability is judged by five factors.

front: What are the five Neil v. Biggers reliability factors for an eyewitness identification (the recognized 5-factor test)?
back: (1) the witness's opportunity to view the criminal at the time of the crime; (2) the witness's degree of attention; (3) the accuracy of the witness's prior description; (4) the level of certainty demonstrated at the confrontation; and (5) the length of time between the crime and the confrontation.

front: Name the first Neil v. Biggers reliability factor.
back: The opportunity of the witness to view the criminal at the time of the crime.

front: Name the second Neil v. Biggers reliability factor.
back: The witness's degree of attention.

front: Name the third Neil v. Biggers reliability factor.
back: The accuracy of the witness's prior description of the criminal.

front: Name the fourth Neil v. Biggers reliability factor.
back: The level of certainty demonstrated by the witness at the confrontation.

front: Name the fifth Neil v. Biggers reliability factor.
back: The length of time between the crime and the confrontation.

front: In Manson v. Brathwaite (1977), what did the Court hold about identifications from unnecessarily suggestive procedures?
back: There is no per se rule of exclusion; reliability is the linchpin, assessed under the Biggers five-factor totality and weighed against the corrupting effect of the suggestive procedure.

front: Manson v. Brathwaite: "reliability is the {{c1::linchpin}} in determining the admissibility of identification testimony for both pre- and post-Stovall confrontations. The factors to be considered are set out in Biggers."
back: linchpin

front: Under Manson v. Brathwaite, against what is an eyewitness ID's reliability weighed once a procedure is found suggestive?
back: Against the corrupting effect of the suggestive procedure itself — the reliability factors are balanced against the degree of suggestion.

front: In Perry v. New Hampshire (2012), when does the Due Process Clause require preliminary judicial screening of an eyewitness ID's reliability?
back: Only when the suggestive circumstances were arranged by law enforcement. Absent improper police conduct, reliability is left to the ordinary trial safeguards — cross-examination, jury instructions, and the jury.

front: Under Perry v. New Hampshire, what is the remedy when suggestiveness arises by chance rather than police arrangement (e.g., a witness's own spontaneous viewing)?
back: Not suppression. The remedy is the ordinary trial safeguards — cross-examination and jury instructions; the due-process screen is not triggered.

front: Common pitfall: instructors assume the Sixth Amendment right to counsel attaches at every lineup. Why is that wrong?
back: The right is limited to post-charge corporeal confrontations. It does not attach at pre-charge showups (Kirby) or at photo arrays (Ash); pre-charge field showups need no defense counsel.

front: Common pitfall: treating any suggestive identification procedure as automatically fatal. Why is that wrong?
back: Under Biggers/Manson, a suggestive ID is still admissible if reliable under the five-factor totality. Suppression is the exception, not the rule — reliability can save it.

front: Common pitfall: applying due-process suppression screening to suggestion that police did not arrange. Why is that wrong?
back: Per Perry, suggestion police did not arrange does not trigger the due-process screen. The remedy is cross-examination and jury instruction, not exclusion — do not invoke the exclusionary rule for chance suggestiveness.

front: Eyewitness ID doctrine: can an identification pass the Sixth Amendment counsel inquiry yet fail the due-process suggestiveness inquiry (or vice versa)?
back: Yes. The two inquiries are independent — the Wade/Gilbert counsel question and the Stovall/Biggers/Manson suggestiveness question are analyzed separately, and an ID can satisfy one while violating the other.
